coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of aspartic acid-derived chiral carbon dots and their effect on bovine serum albumin amyloid fibrillation by multispectral and molecular interaction studies
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Jiawei
Fang, Liping
Li, Jie
Gao, Xuejiao
Li, Dexin
Wang, Sujuan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, two chiral carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using L/D-aspartic acid as a carbon and chirality source via a rapid microwave-assisted one-step method. The prepared chiral CDs were characterized using various analysis techniques. Transmission electron microscopy images showed that the sizes of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2–4 and 1–3 nm, respectively. In circular dichroism spectra, the peaks at 200–220 nm for L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were inherited from L-Asp and D-Asp acid, respectively. In addition, amidation reactions generated new chiral centers. Thioflavin T was used to show that the formation of bovine serum albumin (BSA) amyloid fibril did not involve a nucleation phase, and D-Asp-CDs exhibited a more significant promotion effect on BSA amyloid fibrillation than L-Asp-CDs. Native BSA could form worm-like amyloid fibrils after incubation at 65 °C for 8 h. However, the addition of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs led to fibril networks and larger aggregates, respectively. The promotion effect was attributed to the high local concentration due to the absorption on the surface of CDs. The interaction between BSA and L/D-Asp-CDs was further studied, and the quenching mechanism was static quenching. The complex formed between BSA and D-Asp-CDs was more stable than that formed between BSA and L-Asp-CDs, which explains why D-Asp-CDs promoted BSA amyloid fibrillation more significantly than L-Asp-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral carbon dots
Amyloid fibrillation
Bovine serum albumin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CR binding and ANS fluorescence assay
Synthesis and characterization of chiral CDs
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra measurement
Effect of chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibril formation
Effect of chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibril morphology
Effect of chiral CDs on BSA secondary structure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22202056 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A: L-Asp-CDs; B: D-Asp-CDs) and XRD pattern (C: L-Asp-CDs; D: D-Asp-CDs) of the chiral CDs.
The XPS spectra of the L-Asp-CDs (A–D) and FTIR spectra of L/D-Asp-CDs (E, F).
UV-vis absorption (A, B) and fluorescence spectra (C, D) of the obtained chiral CDs;the circular dichroism (CD) spectra of raw material (E) and obtained chiral CDs (F).

ThT fluorescence spectra of BSA without and with prepared chiral CDs after incubation for 8 hours (A) and the kinetic plot of BSA without and with prepared chiral CDs (B); CR binding absorption spectra (C) and ANS fluorescence spectra (D) of BSA fibril with or without chiral CDs
Transmission electron microscopy images of BSA fibril (A) and BSA solution with addition of L-Asp-CDs (B) and D-Asp-CDs (C) (incubation at 65°C for 8 h).
CD spectra of native BSA and BSA after incubation for 8 h with or without chiral CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of BSA quenched by L-Asp-CDs (A) or D-Asp-CDs (B); Stern-Volmer plot for BSA quenched by L/D-Asp-CDs (C) and Lineweaver-Burk curves for BSA by L/D-Asp-CDs (D).
ITC titration curves of BSA with L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs.

Simulated chemical structure of L-Asp-CDs (A) and D-Asp-CDs (B). The conformation of L-Asp-CDs-BSA (C) and D-Asp-CDs-BSA (E) complexes with the lowest-docking energy. Three-dimensional (3D) images show the interaction between L-Asp-CDs (D) or D-Asp-CDs (F) and its surrounding residues. Hydrogen bonding is denoted by the bright yellow dashed lines.
The synthesis route of chiral CDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of aspartic acid-derived chiral carbon dots and their effect on bovine serum albumin amyloid fibrillation by multispectral and molecular interaction studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, two chiral carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using L/D-aspartic acid as a carbon and chirality source via a rapid microwave-assisted one-step method. The prepared chiral CDs were characterized using various analysis techniques. Transmission electron microscopy images showed that the sizes of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2–4 and 1–3 nm, respectively. In circular dichroism spectra, the peaks at 200–220 nm for L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were inherited from L-Asp and D-Asp acid, respectively. In addition, amidation reactions generated new chiral centers. Thioflavin T was used to show that the formation of bovine serum albumin (BSA) amyloid fibril did not involve a nucleation phase, and D-Asp-CDs exhibited a more significant promotion effect on BSA amyloid fibrillation than L-Asp-CDs. Native BSA could form worm-like amyloid fibrils after incubation at 65 °C for 8 h. However, the addition of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs led to fibril networks and larger aggregates, respectively. The promotion effect was attributed to the high local concentration due to the absorption on the surface of CDs. The interaction between BSA and L/D-Asp-CDs was further studied, and the quenching mechanism was static quenching. The complex formed between BSA and D-Asp-CDs was more stable than that formed between BSA and L-Asp-CDs, which explains why D-Asp-CDs promoted BSA amyloid fibrillation more significantly than L-Asp-CDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs are an emerging subclass of carbonaceous nanoparticles [1,2] that have attracted considerable interest in recent years owing to their biological applications [3,4], including bioimaging, chemical and biological sensing, drug delivery, and antibacterial properties. Their small size, biocompatibility, and water solubility [5] allow them to easily access all parts of the body, including the blood-brain barrier, when they are used in biological and medical fields. The increasing application of CDs in biological and medical field, and their ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, has made the study of the interaction between CDs and proteins critical [6]. In recent years, many efforts have been made to investigate their interactions and understand CD-induced structural and conformational changes in proteins. Previous studies have indicated that the CD-induced conformational changes in proteins could affect the formation of protein amyloid fibrils [7,8]. Many diseases such as Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's diseases and other degenerative diseases have been related to protein conformational changes and amyloid fibril formations. Therefore, studies on the mechanism of amyloid fibril formation and the effect of other substances on amyloid fibril formation remain a hot topic.

Chirality is widespread in nature and has been highly valued by scientists since its discovery [9,10]. Chirality is critical in physiological activities [11,12]. The application of CDs in biological and medical fields would be greatly improved if chirality were introduced [13]. Chiral CDs were first synthesized in 2016 and several studies on their effect on amyloid fibrillation have been reported after their discovery [14,15]. The Raz Jelinek group prepared enantiomeric CDs from L-lysine or D-lysine and demonstrated that CDs from L-lysine could remodel Aβ42 secondary structure and amyloid fibril morphologies [16]. The group also reported a new class of chiral carbon quantum dots using D- or L-lysine as the sole carbonaceous building block with the CDs from L-lysine which exhibits a better ability to modulate the structural transformations and prion peptide (106–126) aggregation [17]. The Xiaoyuan Deng group produced new cysteine-derived chiral carbon quantum dots that were fibrinolytic regulators of plasmin targeting human islet amyloid polypeptide in type 2 diabetes mellitus [18]. However, the effect of chiral CDs on amyloid fibrillation was no longer the subject of system reports, and the mechanism underlying the promotion or inhibition on the amyloid fibrillation remained elusive.

Nanoparticles (NPs) exhibit a high surface-to-volume ratio enabling them to absorb protein in solution and induce high local protein concentrations on their surface. The local high concentration could inhibit or promote protein self-assembly into amyloid fibrils. Some stuides have reported on the inhibition effect of NPs on the formation of amyloid fibrils [19,20]. Other studies have reported that copper oxide [21], silica [22], TiO2 NPs [23,24] could promote the formation of amyloid fibrils. The inhibition or promotion effect of NPs on amyloid fibrillation is significantly related to the protein structure and NPs properties, such as size, surface charge and functional groups. Therefore, the further investigation is necessary to better understand the inhibition or promotion mechanism of different NPs or amyloid-prone protein.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) is a protein with high structural stability that has been extensively studied. BSA consisted of 583 amino acid residues and α-helical with three homologous domains which share 76% sequence homology with human serum albumin [25]. Significantly, both BSA and human serum albumin can transform into amyloid fibrils under special solution conditions [26,27]. However, BSA is an important protein model for studying the process and mechanism of amyloid fibrillation because it is less expensive than human serum albumin.

In this study, two chiral CDs were prepared using a rapid microwave-assisted method with L/D-Aspic acid as the carbon and chirality source. The structure of the prepared chiral CDs was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurement. The CD chirality signals at 200–220 nm were inherited from aspartic acid, whereas new signals at 225–260 nm were generated during the CDs formation. To investigate the synthesized chiral CDs’ dissimilar effect on amyloid fibrillation, BSA was used as a model to perform the experiments. The results demonstrated that both two chiral CDs could promote the formation of BSA amyloid fibrillation with D-Asp-CDs exhibiting more significant promotion effect than L-Asp-CDs. To date, the formation mechanism of amyloid fibrils has been controversial. This study provides new insight into the mechanism and the effect of NPs on the amyloid fibrillation.

L/D-Asp acid was purchased from Rhanwn. NH4HCO3 was purchased from Jindong Tianzheng Precision Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). BSA (purity ≥ 98.0%) was purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Thioflavin T (ThT) was purchased from J&K Chemicals Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Congo red (CR) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. 8-anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (ANS) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphotungstic acid and Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Kermel Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All other materials were of analytical grade.

The CDs were prepared using microwave assisted pyrolysis method as follows. Briefly, L/D-Asp (1 g) and ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3, 2 g) were dissolved in 20 mL ultrapure water in a beaker. After sonicating for 15 min, the beaker was heated using microwave irradiation for 4 min in a home microwave oven (TOSHIBA ER-SS20CNW) operating at a power of 560 W. The obtained solution was filtered with 0.22 µm polyether sulphone membrane. The filtrate was then purified by dialyzing against deionized water using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off =500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove the nonreactive molecules. After that, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain the final chiral CDs.

The size and morphology of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were measured using the TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For TEM characterization, a drop of L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs suspension was deposited on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. XRD spectra of L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs were obtained using an X-ray diffraction spectrometer (D8 ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with CuKα as the source. The FTIR spectra of the samples in KBr pellets was obtained on an FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10, Thermo, USA) equipped with a deuterated triglycine sulfate detector. All FTIR spectra were recorded in the wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a resolution of 0.4 cm₋1 and 32 scans. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were investigated on a UV spectrometer (UV-2550 Shimadzu, Japan). Circular dichroism (CD) spectra were recorded in the range of 190–300 nm on a CD spectrometer (MOS-450/SFM300, Bio-logic, French). XPS measurements were performed on Scientific ESCALAB QXi. Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) measurements were performed using a nano-ITC (TA instruments, USA).

Incubated BSA samples were prepared by dissolving BSA in Tris-HCl buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.4) containing NaCl (140 mMol/L) and NaN3 (v/v, 0.2%). The final concentration of BSA was 3 mg/mL. To investigate the effect of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs on amyloid fibrillation, BSA samples with the same concentration of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were also prepared. All samples were incubated at 65°C for 8 h in a thermal shaker without agitation.

ThT was dissolved into in a phosphate buffer solution (10 mM mM, pH = 7.4) to prepare a stock solution (0.01 mM mM). BSA incubation solution (60 µL) was taken at every hour and was mixed with 2-mL ThT stock solution. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed solutions were measured on RF-5301PC (Shimadu, Japan). Excitation wavelength was set at 450 nm, and the emission intensity at 486 nm was recorded. ThT data were presented as the mean from five independent samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CR binding and ANS fluorescence assay
ce:section.ce:para: CR stock solution (20 µM) was prepared by dissolving CR solid in PB solution (10 mM mM, pH = 7.4) and stored in the refrigerator. Aliquots of BSA samples (140 µL) were incubated for 8 h and mixed with the CR stock solution. The mixed solution was placed in a thermostatic water bath for 30 min, and the absorption spectra were measured on UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550 Shimadzu, Japan), with a scanning wavelength range of 400–700 nm and the slit width of 1 nm. ANS stock solution was prepared by dissolving ANS in phosphate buffer solution and stored in a refrigerator for use. A 100-µL incubated BSA sample was mixed with 2-mL ANS stock solution and the fluorescence spectra were measured on an RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with an excitation wavelength set at 380 nm. CR binding and ANS fluorescence data were also presented as the mean obtained from five independent samples.

BSA samples with or without chiral CDs were incubated at 65°C and pH = 7.4 for 8 h. After incubation, the samples were diluted with pure water and coated copper-mesh grids were entirely dipped into the solution to absorb the sample. The grids were then stained with 1% (w/v) phosphotungstic acid and air-dried at room temperature. Finally, they were observed using a TEM (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA).

BSA solutions with or without chiral CDs were incubated at 65°C for 8h. Then a 16-µL incubated solution was diluted with 1984-µL Tris-HCl buffer solution and CD spectra were recorded using a CD spectrometer (MOS-450/SFM300, Bio-Logic) with a 1.0-cm quartz cell. The scanning rate was set to 0.25 nm/s and the step intervals were set to 0.25 nm.

BSA was dissolved in Tris-HCl buffer solution (20 mM, pH = 7.4) with a concentration of 28 μM. In addition, BSA samples solutions with different concentrations of L/D-Asp-CDs were prepared. The BSA concentration was also 28 μM and the concentration of L/D-Asp-CDs was 600, 900, 1050, 1200 and 1350 μM. The fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured using fluorescence spectrometer (F-7000, HITACHI, Japan) at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm.

The interaction between L/D-Asp-CDs and BSA was measured using Nano-ITC (TA Instruments, USA). Typically, 1 mg/mL L/D-Asp-CDs and 3 mg/mL BSA stock solutions in Tris-HCl (pH = 7.4, 20 mM) were prepared. A 1000-µL BSA solution was loaded into the sample cell and 250-µL L/D-Asp-CDs solution was filled to the syringe. Titration was performed every 300 s for 25 injections of 10 µL each.

The binding information between L/D-Asp-CDs and BSA was performed using Autodock 4.2 software. The chemical structure of BSA was obtained from the research collaborator for structural informatics protein data bank (PDB:4F5S). The possible molecular structures of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were drawn using Chem3D according to the results of the structural analysis. Docking was performed using the Lamarckian genetic algorithm method. Pymol software was used to scale out the binding sites of chiral CDs and BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of chiral CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups on the surface of the chiral CDs were further characterized using XPS (Fig. 2 and S1). Both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs XPS spectroscopy showed three peaks at 284.8, 399.8 and 531.4 eV, attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Figs. 2A and S1A). The C1s spectrum displayed peaks at 284.8, 286 and 288 eV, indicating the presence of C=C, C–N and C=O (Figs. 2B and S1B). The N1s spectrum showed two fitted peaks at 399.8 and 401 eV, assigned to C–N and N–H groups, respectively (Figs. 2C and S1C). The O1s spectrum showed peaks at 531.4 and 532.6 eV, indicating the presence of C–OH and C-O-C (Figs. 2D and S1D).

FTIR spectra of the reactant L/D-Asp (Fig. S2) and prepared chiral CDs (Fig. 2E and 2F) were measured to determine the surface functional groups presented on the obtained chiral CDs. The structures of L/D-Asp and prepared CDs are shown in Scheme 1. The assignment of the most important groups in reactant L/D-Asp and the product CDs were presented as follows.

According to the assignment of amine acid zwitterion in the literature [31–34], the C=O stretching bands for reactants L-Asp and D-Asp were at 1695 and 1691 cm₋1, respectively. The band at 1607 and 1600 cm₋1 correspond to the asymmetric stretching of the COO group in L-Asp and D-Asp, respectively. The C–O stretching for L-Asp and D-Asp were both at 1248 cm₋1. The bands at 1716 cm₋1 in the IR spectra of both chiral CDs contributed to C=O stretching vibration, confirming the presence of C=O in the structure of both prepared chiral CDs.

Regarding the NH3 assignment for the zwitterion reported by Cao and Fischer [35], the bands at 3422 and 3403 cm₋1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of L-Asp and D-Asp, respectively. Symmetric deformation for L-Asp and D-Asp was observed at 1514 and 1511 cm₋1, respectively. The C-N stretching at 1400 cm₋1 in both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs spectra indicated an amidation reaction between the NH3 and COO groups during the formation process of CDs. The surface of CDs had only few NH3 groups because of the formation of a carbon core. Therefore, the NH3 bands were screened using the band of OH groups at 3430 cm₋1 in L-Asp-CDs and 3432 cm₋1 in D-Asp-CDs. However, the N–H bands for L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were found at 1637 and 1640 cm₋1, respectively, demonstrating the presence of NH3 groups on the surface of chiral CDs [36].

For L-Asp and D-Asp, the symmetric stretching vibration of the CH2 group was observed at 2957 and 2956 cm₋1 , respectively. The bands at 2871 and 2872 cm₋1 were contributed to the symmetric stretching vibration of C-H in L-Asp and D-Asp, respectively. However, only weak bands at 2950 cm₋1 for L-Asp-CDs and 2975 cm₋1 for D-Asp-CDs contributed to the stretching of CH2 groups because of the carbonization reaction in the preparation of CDs.

Based on the above FTIR analysis, nitrogen and oxygen atoms were successfully doped into the CDs. Furthermore, similar absorption positions for L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs revealed that they possessed similar functional groups. The surface components of the obtained chiral CDs confirmed by the FTIR analysis were highly consistent with the results of XPS spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra measurement
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were used to investigate the optical properties of the prepared chiral CDs. Fig. 3A and 3B showed a broad absorption peak around 280–300 nm, indicating the presence of n→π* transition in both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs. The fluorescence emission wavelength for both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs was red-shifted with the excitation wavelength changing from 300 to 400 nm (Fig. 3C and 3D). The maximum emission fluorescence for L-Asp-CDs is 350 and 370 nm for D-Asp-CDs.

Fig. 3E and 3F shows the results of CD spectra used to investigate the chiral properties of the obtained CDs. L-Asp had a positive peak and D-Asp had a negative one. The two peaks were approximately 197 nm and symmetrical. Comparing the spectra of L/D-Asp-CDs with that of raw material L/D-Asp, the CD signals of chiral CDs at 200–220 nm were inherited from L-Asp and D-Asp, respectively. The red shift in the CD signal could be due to the group change after interaction between L/D-Asp and carbon skeletons. In addition, a new symmetrical broad CD signal at 225–260 nm appeared, indicating that a new chiral center was generated because of the amidation reaction during the formation process of CDs [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibril formation
ce:section.ce:para:
BSA, as a nondisease protein, has been extensively used as a model protein to study the formation and growth of amyloid fibrils owing to its 76% sequence homology with human serum albumin and low price compared with human serum albumin. Moreover, BSA could be easily induced to form amyloid fibrils under various solution conditions. Herein, BSA was incubated under pH = 7.4 at 65°C with 140 mM NaCl and 0.2% NaN3 (v/v) to form amyloid fibrils. The growth process of amyloid fibrils was characterized by measuring the change in ThT fluorescence intensity because ThT specially binds with highly ordered β-sheet of mature amyloid fibrils. Fig. 4A showed the ThT fluorescence spectra of BSA samples without or with prepared chiral CDs after 8 h. The spectra showed that the final quantity of mature amyloid fibrils with chiral CDs was more than that of BSA samples without chiral CDs and the samples with D-Asp-CDs were more than that with L-Asp-CDs at the same concentration ratio (20:1). This result demonstrated that D-Asp-CDs were superior to L-Asp-CDs in promoting the formation of BSA amyloid fibrils. The fluorescence intensity at different incubation time was fitted using the following equation (Eq. 1) [38]:
(1)
F =
F 0
+
F ∞
(
1 −
e
− r t
)

In this equation, F is the ThT fluorescence intensity at different incubation time, F0 is the ThT fluorescence intensity at t = 0, F∞ is the ThT fluorescence intensity after prolonged incubation time, r is the rate constant, and t is the incubation time. The fitted kinetic curve of BSA without or with chiral CDs at different incubation times was shown in Fig. 4B. The growth process of BSA amyloid fibrils contained two stages, namely, rapid growth stag and plateau stags. The lack of a lag phase indicated that nucleation was not involved in the formation of BSA amyloid fibrils. The ThT fluorescence intensity immediately increased after incubation of all samples with or without chiral CDs. The intensity reached the maximum value at the same time (2 h), suggesting that both D-Asp-CDs and L-Asp-CDs did not change the growth rate of BSA amyloid fibril. The final amyloid fibril quantity of the sample with D-Asp-CDs was significantly more than that of the sample without chiral CDs, indicating that D-Asp-CDs significantly promote BSA amyloid fibrillation and L-Asp-CDs slightly promote BSA amyloid fibrillation. Overall, D-Asp-CDs exhibited a more pronounced promotion effect on BSA amyloid fibrillation than L-Asp-CDs.

As a supplement to the evaluation of amyloid fibrillation, CR absorption spectroscopy was used to probe the presence of cross β-sheet in amyloid fibrils and the results are shown in Fig. 4C. The maximum absorption peak of CR was at 497 nm, but after binding with BSA fibril, it was red-shifted to 505 nm. When BSA with the same concentration of L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs was incubated for 8 h, the CR absorbance was notably increased. This result showed that both chiral CDs could promote the formation of BSA amyloid fibrils, with D-Asp-CDs showing a more pronounced effect than L-Asp-CDs.

In the process of amyloid fibril formation, exposure to hydrophobic surfaces was an important driving force. ANS exhibits weak fluorescence in an aqueous environment, but its intensity enhances significantly upon binding with the hydrophobic surface of proteins. Therefore, ANS is commonly used as a hydrophobic probe to detect the change of hydrophobicity during the formation process of amyloid fibrils. The ANS fluorescence spectra of native BSA at different incubation times are shown in Fig. S3. ANS fluorescence intensity decreased significantly within the first hour and then remained stable with prolonged incubation time. BSA possesses hydrophobic cavities in its structure, which were embedded in the interior of the molecular chains with the formation of amyloid fibrils. This result was not consistent with the amyloid formation process of other proteins [39,40]. After L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs were added to BSA incubation samples, the formation of amyloid fibrils was promoted and both ANS fluorescence intensities were increased accordingly (Fig. 4D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibril morphology
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to visually observe the effect of chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibril morphology (Fig. 5 ). After incubation without chiral CDs at 65°C for 8 h, worm-like amyloid fibrils were formed. However, under the same experimental conditions, the presence of L-Asp-CDs formed self-assembled fibril networks. Furthermore, larger aggregates were formed when D-Asp-CDs were added to the BSA incubation sample. These results indicated that the formation of amyloid fibrils was accelerated during the co-incubation of BSA with chiral CDs under amyloidogenic conditions, and D-Asp-CDs showed a more significant promotion effect than L-Asp-CDs. This result was consistent with the spectroscopic measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of chiral CDs on BSA secondary structure
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of amyloid fibrils is invariably linked to a conformation change from α-helical to β-sheet, which can be detected using CD absorption spectroscopy. Fig. 6 shows the CD spectra of native BSA and BSA after incubation for 8 h with or without chiral CDs. The native BSA exhibited two strong negative peaks around 210 and 225 nm, indicating that BSA contained a significant amount of α-helical conformation in its native state. After BSA was incubated at 65°C for 8 h, the peaks around 210 and 225 nm decreased, indicating that α-helical may change to β-sheet which can lead to the formation of amyloid fibrils. Both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs led to a more significant decrease in α-helical peaks, demonstrating their ability to enhance amyloid fibril formations.

The fluorescence emission spectra of BSA with or without different concentrations of L/D-Asp-CDs are shown in Fig. 7A and 7B, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectrum of BSA without CDs exhibited an emission maximum of 343 nm. The emission intensity of BSA decreased with a gradual increase in L/D-Asp-CDs concentration. Before the analysis of the quenching mechanism, the observed emission intensity should be corrected to eliminate the interference of the inner-filter effect (IFE) using the following equation (Eq. 2) [41]:
(2)
F
c o r
=
F
o b s
×
e
(
A e x + A e m
)
2
where, Fcor and Fobs are the corrected and observed emission intensity, respectively. Aex and Aem are the absorption values of L/D-Asp-CDs at the excitation (260 nm) and emission (343 nm) wavelength, respectively.

The curves of both corrected and observed emission intensity vs. increasing concentration of L/D-Asp-CDs are shown in Fig. S4. After the removal of IFE, the corrected emission intensity still decreased as the concentration of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs increased, indicating that both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs could quench the fluorescence of BSA. The quenching mechanism is usually classified into two: dynamic quenching and static quenching. Dynamic quenching occurs when an excited-state fluorophore is deactivated upon contact with quencher molecules. Static quenching refers to the formation of a ground state complex between the fluorophore and quencher molecule. To predict the possible quenching mechanism, the fluorescence quenching data were analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation (Eq. 3) [42]:
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where F0 is the fluorescence emission intensity of BSA; F is the fluorescence intensity of BSA in the presence of L/D-Asp-CDs; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of L/D-Asp-CDs. The plot was not a linear curve (Fig. 7C), indicating that the quenching mechanism was static quenching rather than dynamic. The fluorescence data were further analyzed using equation (Eq. 4) to calculate the binding constant [43].
(4)
1
F 0
− F
=
1 F
+
1
[ Q ]
F 0
K A
the definitions of F, F0 and [Q] were the same as that in Eq. 3. Fig. 7D shows a curve with longitudinal coordinates of 1/(F0 -F) and horizontal ordinates of 1/[Q]F0 . The binding constant KA was calculated using the slope of the curve, and the binding constant for D-Asp-CDs (1.65 × 103 L/mol) was higher than that for L-Asp-CDs (1.56 ×103 L/mol), indicating that the complex formed between D-Asp-CDs and BSA was more stable.

ITC was used to better understand the molecular interaction between synthesized chiral CDs and BSA. ITC is an important method for studying the biothermodynamics and biodynamics, which is usually used to detect the enthalpy change, entropy change and binding affinity between ligand and protein molecules. ITC spectra were obtained by titrating L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs with BSA solution and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . The interaction between L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs with BSA was both exothermic. The exothermic peak of D-Asp-CDs was higher than that of L-Asp-CDs, indicating that the interaction of D-Asp-CDs with BSA was more stable and easier. The thermodynamic parameters calculated from ITC data are given in Table S1. Based on these thermodynamic parameters, the binding of D-Asp-CDs with BSA was easier and more stable than that of L-Asp-CDs.

Autodock software was used to perform molecular docking to investigate the interaction and binding sites between synthesized chiral CDs and BSA. Based on the structure analysis, a possible chemical structure of synthesized chiral CDs was proposed and depicted in Fig. 9A and 9B. The most stable complexes of L-Asp-CDs-BSA and D-Asp-CDs-BSA are shown in Fig. 9C and 9E, respectively. The binding energies of L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs were –3.25 Kcal/mol and –4.04 Kcal/mol, respectively, indicating that the interaction between D-Asp-CDs and BSA was more stable. This result was consistent with that obtained from ITC. The binding sites of L-Asp-CDs with BSA were Arg439, Glu494 and Lys534 (Fig. 9D), while the binding sites of D-Asp-CDs with BSA were Lys114, Pro117, Asp118, Glu182, Arg185 and Thr518 (Fig. 9F). The binding of BSA with L-Asp-CDs or D-Asp-CDs caused a change in the molecular chain conformation of BSA, which in turn induced a further effect on BSA amyloid fibrillation.

Based on the analysis results of fluorescence quenching, ITC, molecular docking, both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs could interact with BSA, resulting in the formation of a new complex. The interaction of BSA with CDs may induce a higher concentration of BSA in the local solution environment around CDs. The promotion effect of both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs on BSA amyloid fibrillation could be due to the higher concentration of BSA. In addition, the promotion effect of D-Asp-CDs was superior to that of L-Asp-CDs, which could be explained by the interaction of D-Asp-CDs with BSA was more stable and easier than that of L-Asp-CDs.

In this study, two chiral CDs (L-Asp-CDs/D-Asp-CDs) were successfully synthesized via rapid microwave-assisted one-step method using L/D-Asp as the sole carbon and chiral source. The obtained CDs had similar structures and physical properties except for their chirality. The dissimilar effect of two chiral CDs on BSA amyloid fibrillation was investigated using various analytical techniques. These results indicated that both chiral CDs could promote BSA amyloid fibrillation and the promotion effect of D-Asp-CDs was superior to that of L-Asp-CDs. To explain the different effects of the two CDs on BSA amyloid fibrillation, their interactions were studied using fluorescence quenching, ITC measurement, and molecular docking. The results showed that both L-Asp-CDs and D-Asp-CDs could interact with BSA to form new complex, with the interaction of D-Asp-CDs with BSA being easier forming a more stable complex than that of L-Asp-CDs. The binding of BSA with CDs increased the concentration of BSA in the local environment around CDs which further promoted the formation of amyloid fibrils. This phenomenon has also been reported by other researchers. For example, the Jingjun Lyu group reported that CuO NPs could promote the fibrillation process of α-synuclein [21]. The O. PopGeorgievski group investigated the influence of single-walled carbon nanotubes, fullerene carbon quantum dots, and nanodiamonds on hen egg white lysozyme (HEWL) amyloid fibrillation and found that they promoted HEWL amyloid fibrillation at all concentrations [44]. In addition, there have been other reports on the inhibition effect of chiral CDs on protein amyloid fibrillation. The Raz Jelinek group synthesized two chiral CDs using D- or L-lysine as the sole carbonaceous building block and found that they could inhibit prion peptide and Aβ42 amyloid fibrillation [16]. The Chen-Qiao Li group used L- or D-cysteine to prepare high-oxygen CDs and found that they significantly inhibited human insulin amyloid fibrillation [45]. These results differ from our experimental results in this work. Understanding whether NPs inhibit or promote amyloid fibrillation is a critical problem that requires much research. The effectiveness of NPs may be correlated with their size, charge, end group and the molecular structure of the protein. Therefore, the result of this study provides insights into the complex interaction of CDs with proteins and offers substantial insight into the formation of different protein amyloid fibrils. This finding also raises concerns about potential harm of some carbon dots to our health. Therefore, reducing the intake of some foods containing CDs may reduce the risk of amyloid fibrosis disease.

Jiawei Dong: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Liping Fang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jie Li: Investigation, Data curation. Xuejiao Gao: Data curation, Investigation. Dexin Li: Data curation, Investigation. Sujuan Wang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22202056).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.136045.
